text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
Northern General Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Herries Road, Sheffield S5 7AU  

Dear Dr Patel,  

James Walker, DOB: 14-08-1990, Hospital No: S947213 NHS No. 8035162947  
12 Millstone Close, Sheffield, S6 2QP  

Diagnoses: 

Generalised epilepsy. 

Thank you for asking me to review James Walker in the neurology clinic. He attended alone today and works in a manual, safety‑sensitive role involving heights and intermittent use of powered machinery. He remains on sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. Three weeks ago his GP increased levetiracetam to 1.25 g twice daily following two brief morning absences at work. Since that change, James Walker reports that his typical events remain generalised tonic‑clonic seizures occurring predominantly from sleep, with occasional brief absence episodes during the day. He keeps a diary and notes: "Frequency increased by ~20% after dose increase." This has been over a 3‑week observation window and coincided with a run of night shifts. No injuries reported, but one witnessed near‑miss on a gantry when he felt pre‑syncopal and stepped back as a precaution. There is no new head injury, systemic illness, or missed doses. Sleep deprivation is likely contributory. No rescue medication used. 

On examination today he was well, cardiorespiratory exam normal, and neurological examination unremarkable between events. Weight 82 kg, BP 124/76, pulse 72 regular. No tremor or ataxia. 

Investigations: Routine bloods (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) last week were within normal limits. Levetiracetam level not measured; valproate level mid‑therapeutic range per GP record. Prior MRI brain (2022) normal. EEG (2023) showed generalised spike‑and‑wave discharges consistent with generalised epilepsy. 

Plan discussed with James Walker: He will continue his current prescribed regimen for now while we clarify whether the observed increase is a transient fluctuation related to shift pattern and sleep loss. He will maintain a detailed seizure/symptom diary including sleep hours, shifts worked, and any near‑misses. We have arranged an urgent occupational health review through his employer regarding task allocation around heights and machinery. I have requested a trough levetiracetam level before morning dose in two weeks, alongside repeat LFTs. We will bring forward his routine EEG to assess for any change in interictal activity and schedule a telephone review in three weeks to reassess frequency and tolerability. He understands DVLA guidance remains applicable as previously advised. 

Safety: We discussed practical risk reduction at work and at home, including ensuring colleagues are aware of basic first aid for seizures, avoiding lone working at height, and using fall‑arrest where mandated by site policy. He carries an ID card and has consented to me copying this letter to occupational health. 

I will keep you updated and would be happy to review sooner if there are further events or concerns. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He keeps a diary and notes: "Frequency increased by ~20% after dose increase."
Frequency increased by ~20% after dose increase

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter confirms ongoing seizures (generalised tonic-clonic predominantly from sleep, with occasional daytime absences). It describes a 3-week observation window after a medication change and notes a ~20% increase in frequency, but provides no explicit numeric count of seizures or a rate per day/week/month/year. The mention of “two brief morning absences” refers to events preceding the dose increase and does not establish a current frequency. Terms like “occasional” lack a defined time unit. Therefore, a precise current seizure frequency cannot be determined from the letter.
seizure_frequency_number: He keeps a diary and notes: "Frequency increased by ~20% after dose increase."
reference: Frequency increased by ~20% after dose increase
